Authors: Wanping Ma; Hanjie Wen; Zhengbing Zhou; Fei Xu; Chuanwei Zhu; Lin Xu

Journal: Journal of Geochemical Exploration

DOI: 10.1016/j.gexplo.2026.108057

Relevance: 0.558 (consider)

Rhenium (Re) is a critical metal, predominantly concentrated in molybdenite through lattice substitution, with more than 90% of global production sourced from porphyry deposits. Although fluid boiling is a fundamental ore-forming process in porphyry systems, the role of vapor and liquid phases derived from boiling in controlling Re enrichment remains poorly constrained. Recent studies indicate that Re can partition significantly into the vapor phase and be effectively transported within it; vapor is also known to preferentially incorporate heavy Mo isotopes. Here, the Re contents, Mo isotopic compositions of molybdenite, and thermodynamic modeling results for the Jinduicheng porphyry Mo deposit in the East Qinling–Dabie Mo belt are reported. Our data show that Re concentrations in molybdenite range from 7.04 to 34.24 ppm, and δ 98 Mo values vary from +0.66‰ to −0.38‰. A systematic decrease in δ 98 Mo values over the hydrothermal sequence indicates Mo isotope fractionation driven by fluid boiling. The negative correlation between Re content and δ 98 Mo suggests that Re preferentially partitions into the vapor phase over Mo and precipitates later during cooling, resulting in late-stage, Re-rich molybdenite. Thermodynamic simulations support this boiling-controlled partitioning and precipitation behavior. Furthermore, boiling-induced salinity increase in the residual liquid enhances Re complexation with chloride and promotes Re mobility, consistent with the negative correlation between Re/Mo and δ 98 Mo. Our study demonstrates that Mo isotopes serve as a sensitive tracer for Re transport and enrichment processes in porphyry systems, providing new insights into the mechanisms of Re enrichment.

item.title: Regional‐to‐Global Tectonic and Trench‐Morphology Effects of Oceanic Plateau Subduction
item.dc:creator: Yang Liu
Nicolas Coltice
Laetitia Le Pourhiet
Ziyin Wu
item.description: Abstract Regional geodynamic modeling has shown that oceanic plateau subduction can modify the slab behavior. However, few studies have explored these interactions beyond 100 Myr in 3‐D spherical models. Using 3‐D spherical mantle convection models with self‐consistent plate‐like behavior, we investigate how the geometry and rheological properties of oceanic plateaus influence trench evolution and mantle flow across regional to global scales. Our models show that plateau width and thickness exert the strongest control on trench retreat, while buoyancy alone plays a secondary role. A mechanically strong plateau produces a transient arcuate trench morphology that gradually heals once the plateau is fully subducted, leaving only a delay in retreat. The pronounced curvature of the northern Mariana Trench suggests that the Ogasawara Plateau is mechanically strong. Although plateau subduction leaves no long‐term geological scar unless slab break‐off occurs, it substantially slows trench migration and can influence global tectonic evolution for over 200 Myr. Published: 2026-03-27T11:35:12+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.557 (ignore)

Authors: Yang Liu; Nicolas Coltice; Laetitia Le Pourhiet; Ziyin Wu

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2025gl120357

Relevance: 0.557 (ignore)

Abstract Regional geodynamic modeling has shown that oceanic plateau subduction can modify the slab behavior. However, few studies have explored these interactions beyond 100 Myr in 3‐D spherical models. Using 3‐D spherical mantle convection models with self‐consistent plate‐like behavior, we investigate how the geometry and rheological properties of oceanic plateaus influence trench evolution and mantle flow across regional to global scales. Our models show that plateau width and thickness exert the strongest control on trench retreat, while buoyancy alone plays a secondary role. A mechanically strong plateau produces a transient arcuate trench morphology that gradually heals once the plateau is fully subducted, leaving only a delay in retreat. The pronounced curvature of the northern Mariana Trench suggests that the Ogasawara Plateau is mechanically strong. Although plateau subduction leaves no long‐term geological scar unless slab break‐off occurs, it substantially slows trench migration and can influence global tectonic evolution for over 200 Myr.

item.title: From dry to damp lunar mantle: How trace water in orthopyroxene reshapes the internal thermal state of the Moon
item.dc:creator: Xinxin Yan
Xinzhuan Guo
Yun Zhou
Yuping Song
Qingshan Zhang
Meng Lv
item.description: The thermal conductivity and diffusivity of mantle minerals fundamentally control planetary cooling rates. Orthopyroxene is a major constituent of the lunar mantle, yet the influence of trace water on its thermophysical properties under high-pressure and high-temperature conditions relevant to the lunar interior has remained unquantified. Here, we present high P – T measurements of these properties for synthetic enstatite containing 0–427 ppm H 2 O using an enhanced transient plane source method. Our results demonstrate that even trace water drastically reduces thermal transport efficiency by enhancing phonon scattering. Incorporating these data into lunar thermal evolution models reveals that a hydrated mantle maintains significantly higher internal temperatures than an anhydrous system over geologic time. By reconciling our model geotherms with the solidus of various lunar mantle constituents and with seismic constraints on the largely solid modern mantle, we constrain the bulk water content of the lunar mantle to around 300 ppm. This work redefines the thermal state of the Moon and provides a critical mechanism for explaining its prolonged magmatic evolution. Published: 2026-03-29T07:08:20+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.556 (ignore)

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120009

Relevance: 0.556 (ignore)

The thermal conductivity and diffusivity of mantle minerals fundamentally control planetary cooling rates. Orthopyroxene is a major constituent of the lunar mantle, yet the influence of trace water on its thermophysical properties under high-pressure and high-temperature conditions relevant to the lunar interior has remained unquantified. Here, we present high P – T measurements of these properties for synthetic enstatite containing 0–427 ppm H 2 O using an enhanced transient plane source method. Our results demonstrate that even trace water drastically reduces thermal transport efficiency by enhancing phonon scattering. Incorporating these data into lunar thermal evolution models reveals that a hydrated mantle maintains significantly higher internal temperatures than an anhydrous system over geologic time. By reconciling our model geotherms with the solidus of various lunar mantle constituents and with seismic constraints on the largely solid modern mantle, we constrain the bulk water content of the lunar mantle to around 300 ppm. This work redefines the thermal state of the Moon and provides a critical mechanism for explaining its prolonged magmatic evolution.

item.title: Cadmium isotope systematics in seep carbonates: Constraints on depth-dependent partitioning and paleoenvironmental implications
item.dc:creator: Qiaohui Zhong
Zice Jia
Lu Yin
Chifeng He
Zhiyong Lin
Xudong Wang
Qianyong Liang
Dong Feng
Jie Li
item.description: Cadmium (Cd) isotopes are emerging tracers of marine biogeochemical cycling, but their incorporation into seep carbonates—abundant yet underutilized archives in the sedimentary record—remains poorly constrained. Here we report Cd contents and δ 114/110 Cd values of seep carbonates from the South China Sea and the Gulf of Mexico. Sequential extraction experiments demonstrate that Cd is predominantly hosted in carbonate minerals rather than in sulfides, organic matter, or Fe Mn oxides. Distinct δ 114/110 Cd signatures are observed across carbonate mineral types, indicating that formation depth rather than mineralogy primarily controls Cd isotope partitioning in seep carbonates. Specifically, aragonite is isotopically lighter than or similar to bottom seawater, high-Mg calcite (HMC) exhibits δ 114/110 Cd values consistent with bottom seawater, whereas dolomite exhibits lower values than co-occurring HMC. This distribution pattern is primarily controlled by the depth of the sulfate-methane transition zone rather than carbonate mineral facies. Because aragonite and HMC form at shallow seafloor depths while dolomite precipitates at greater depths, the δ 114/110 Cd values of seep carbonates provide valuable records of pore-water conditions during carbonate formation. Compared with inorganic precipitation experiments, seep carbonates show a reduced magnitude of Cd isotopic fractionation, implying stronger controls from pore-water processes in natural settings. These results indicate that aragonite- and HMC-dominated seep carbonates can archive bottom seawater Cd isotopes, whereas dolomite preferentially records deeper pore-water signals. Seep carbonates thus provide a complementary archive for reconstructing Cd cycling and bottom seawater–pore-water gradients in early diagenetic environments. Published: 2026-03-26T00:48:05+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.555 (ignore)

Cadmium (Cd) isotopes are emerging tracers of marine biogeochemical cycling, but their incorporation into seep carbonates—abundant yet underutilized archives in the sedimentary record—remains poorly constrained. Here we report Cd contents and δ 114/110 Cd values of seep carbonates from the South China Sea and the Gulf of Mexico. Sequential extraction experiments demonstrate that Cd is predominantly hosted in carbonate minerals rather than in sulfides, organic matter, or Fe Mn oxides. Distinct δ 114/110 Cd signatures are observed across carbonate mineral types, indicating that formation depth rather than mineralogy primarily controls Cd isotope partitioning in seep carbonates. Specifically, aragonite is isotopically lighter than or similar to bottom seawater, high-Mg calcite (HMC) exhibits δ 114/110 Cd values consistent with bottom seawater, whereas dolomite exhibits lower values than co-occurring HMC. This distribution pattern is primarily controlled by the depth of the sulfate-methane transition zone rather than carbonate mineral facies. Because aragonite and HMC form at shallow seafloor depths while dolomite precipitates at greater depths, the δ 114/110 Cd values of seep carbonates provide valuable records of pore-water conditions during carbonate formation. Compared with inorganic precipitation experiments, seep carbonates show a reduced magnitude of Cd isotopic fractionation, implying stronger controls from pore-water processes in natural settings. These results indicate that aragonite- and HMC-dominated seep carbonates can archive bottom seawater Cd isotopes, whereas dolomite preferentially records deeper pore-water signals. Seep carbonates thus provide a complementary archive for reconstructing Cd cycling and bottom seawater–pore-water gradients in early diagenetic environments.

item.title: Hydrothermal chlorite geochemistry and formation mechanisms: Linking alterations to uranium mineralization and exploration in the Daliang deposit of South China
item.dc:creator: Kunhe Zhu
Xiaodong Liu
Kaixing Wang
Hang Yang
Xinyi Zhou
Liqiang Sun
Jingcun Shi
item.description: Chlorite is the dominant hydrothermal mineral in the Daliang deposit, a granite-related hydrothermal uranium deposit in South China. Despite the prevalence of chlorite, its characteristics and link to uranium mineralization have largely been neglected. Through comprehensive petrographic and geochemical analyses, we identified five distinct chlorite types (Chl-1 to Chl-5) and traced their formation mechanisms (dissolution−precipitation, layer-by-layer replacement, and dissolution−transport−precipitation) and crystallization temperatures (166−303 °C). Our analyses reveal that the chlorite compositions in the Daliang deposit effectively track the distinct evolutionary trends of the physicochemical properties of hydrothermal fluids across different paragenetic stages, including pH and oxygen fugacity. These observations suggest that the intragroup compositional variations in chlorite are primarily controlled by temperature. In contrast, the intergroup differences mainly reflect precursor mineralogy, fluid pH, and variations in oxygen fugacity. The formation of pre-ore chlorites (Chl-1 to Chl-3) provided favorable spatial conditions for uranium mineralization, whereas the evolution of mineralizing fluids from relatively oxidizing conditions (Chl-4) during the early ore stage to reducing conditions (Chl-5) during the main ore stage directly drove the pitchblende formation process. This research establishes new geochemical indicators for exploring uranium. Notably, elevated Cu/Li and Cu/Ga ratios—coupled with decreasing Li/U ratios and high U and Cu concentrations in ore-stage chlorite—emerge as effective proximal vectors for mineralization within the Daliang deposit. These results not only increase our understanding of granite-related hydrothermal uranium deposits but also provide practical tools for accelerating future exploration efforts. Published: 2026-03-30T15:27:18+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.551 (ignore)

Our analyses reveal that the chlorite compositions in the Daliang deposit effectively track the distinct evolutionary trends of the physicochemical properties of hydrothermal fluids across different paragenetic stages, including pH and oxygen fugacity. These observations suggest that the intragroup compositional variations in chlorite are primarily controlled by temperature. In contrast, the intergroup differences mainly reflect precursor mineralogy, fluid pH, and variations in oxygen fugacity. The formation of pre-ore chlorites (Chl-1 to Chl-3) provided favorable spatial conditions for uranium mineralization, whereas the evolution of mineralizing fluids from relatively oxidizing conditions (Chl-4) during the early ore stage to reducing conditions (Chl-5) during the main ore stage directly drove the pitchblende formation process. Notably, elevated Cu/Li and Cu/Ga ratios—coupled with decreasing Li/U ratios and high U and Cu concentrations in ore-stage chlorite—emerge as effective proximal vectors for mineralization within the Daliang deposit. These results not only increase our understanding of granite-related hydrothermal uranium deposits but also provide practical tools for accelerating future exploration efforts.

item.title: Genesis of alkaline trachytes at the northern North China Block and their constraints on the Paleo-Asian Ocean closure and the Pangea assembly
item.dc:creator: Yinchuan Wang
Zuochen Li
Xianzhi Pei
Ruiguang Wang
Yan Ai
Shaowei Zhao
Hai Zhou
Xiao Wang
item.description: The closure of the Paleo-Asian Ocean is pivotal for deciphering Late Paleozoic-Early Mesozoic paleogeographic evolution and Pangea’s assembly, yet the timing and geodynamic mechanism of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt’s final closure remain highly debated. This study comprehensively analyzes petrology, geochemistry, zircon geochronology, and Hf isotopes of Early Permian-Late Triassic trachytes and Early Permian amphibolite in Shangyi (northern North China Block) to constrain its petrogenesis and geodynamics. Zircon U-Pb dating research displays that the three trachytes were formed at 274 Ma, 249 Ma, and 235 Ma, respectively. Geochemically, they are distinguished by high alkali content, FeOT/MgO, and Ga/Al ratio, low MgO, TiO 2 , and P 2 O 5 . They are enriched in large ion lithophile elements (e.g., Rb, Ba, and K) and light rare earth elements (e.g., La, Ce, and Sm). The zircon saturation temperatures (T Zr ) of the whole rock are 945℃, 892℃, and 883℃, respectively, showing A-type granite affinity. The trachyte ε Hf ( t ) isotopes reveal that it is formed by the partial melting of the Archean-Paleoproterozoic crust materials. The amphibolite is dated at 297 Ma. The Permian amphibolite and trachyte were formed in the back-arc extensional tectonic environment caused by slab break-off during the PAO slab subduction to the northern North China Block (NCB). The Triassic trachytes were formed in the extensional stage after the collision between the NCB and the Siberian Block. Thus, the main body of East Asian blocks joined Pangea after the PAO closure in the Late Permian. Published: 2026-03-28T16:15:01+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.549 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yinchuan Wang; Zuochen Li; Xianzhi Pei; Ruiguang Wang; Yan Ai; Shaowei Zhao; Hai Zhou; Xiao Wang

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.jseaes.2026.107071

Relevance: 0.549 (ignore)

The closure of the Paleo-Asian Ocean is pivotal for deciphering Late Paleozoic-Early Mesozoic paleogeographic evolution and Pangea’s assembly, yet the timing and geodynamic mechanism of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt’s final closure remain highly debated. This study comprehensively analyzes petrology, geochemistry, zircon geochronology, and Hf isotopes of Early Permian-Late Triassic trachytes and Early Permian amphibolite in Shangyi (northern North China Block) to constrain its petrogenesis and geodynamics. Zircon U-Pb dating research displays that the three trachytes were formed at 274 Ma, 249 Ma, and 235 Ma, respectively. Geochemically, they are distinguished by high alkali content, FeOT/MgO, and Ga/Al ratio, low MgO, TiO 2 , and P 2 O 5 . They are enriched in large ion lithophile elements (e.g., Rb, Ba, and K) and light rare earth elements (e.g., La, Ce, and Sm). The zircon saturation temperatures (T Zr ) of the whole rock are 945℃, 892℃, and 883℃, respectively, showing A-type granite affinity. The trachyte ε Hf ( t ) isotopes reveal that it is formed by the partial melting of the Archean-Paleoproterozoic crust materials. The amphibolite is dated at 297 Ma. The Permian amphibolite and trachyte were formed in the back-arc extensional tectonic environment caused by slab break-off during the PAO slab subduction to the northern North China Block (NCB). The Triassic trachytes were formed in the extensional stage after the collision between the NCB and the Siberian Block. Thus, the main body of East Asian blocks joined Pangea after the PAO closure in the Late Permian.

item.title: Late Devonian Mn mineralization as a result of volcanism- and orbital-forced palaeoceanographic changes
item.dc:creator: Yinqiang Zhao
Chenchen Yang
Hai Xu
Sha Jiang
Shangguo Zhou
Qizuan Zhang
Runsheng Yin
item.description: Sedimentary Mn carbonate deposits (SMCDs) constitute the dominant global reservoir of Mn, a critical metal essential for modern technologies, yet their formation timing and links to environmental perturbations remain elusive owing to sparse datable minerals and coarse chronostratigraphic resolution. Here, we report astronomical cycle signals preserved in P (indicative of marine productivity) and (Zr+K)/Al (indicative of detrital flux and relative sea level) from drill core ZK2407 of the Xialei SMCD (∼143 Mt Mn), South China, which is embedded in Late Devonian platform/slope facies strata of the Youjiang Basin. Leveraging the 405 kyr eccentricity cycle and conodont biostratigraphy, the astronomical timescale for the Xialei SMCD was established. The deposition of ore layers I and II+III in the Xialei SMCD took ∼130 and ∼550 kyr, respectively. Ore layer I aligns precisely with the Late Famennian Annulata Event (∼363.4 Ma), while ore layer II+III coincides with the Dasberg Event (∼361.9 Ma). As both mass extinctions were volcanically driven, these temporal couplings suggest large volcanic eruptions as the primary forcing for Mn metallogeny. Massive CO2 emissions from large volcanism induced global warming and sea-level rise, fostering redox-stratified oceanic conditions conducive to SMCD formation. Mn concentrations covary positively in-phase with P and sea-level, indicating transgression-mediated co-transport of anoxic deep-sea hydrothermal Mn(II) and nutrients to the oxic shallow sea, where Mn(III/IV) oxides precipitated. The resulting heightened productivity drove organic matter burial, which subsequently facilitated the reductive transformation of these oxides into Mn carbonates during early diagenesis. Orbital forcing modulated the rhythm of Mn enrichment on astronomical timescales by periodically driving paleoenvironmental variations. This work delineates a temporal framework for the formation of SMCDs and demonstrates that the metallogeny of SMCDs resulted from paleoceanographic changes induced by large volcanism and orbital dynamics. Published: 2026-03-27T12:17:45+00:00 Venue: Science China Earth Sciences Score: 0.545 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yinqiang Zhao; Chenchen Yang; Hai Xu; Sha Jiang; Shangguo Zhou; Qizuan Zhang; Runsheng Yin

Journal: Science China Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1007/s11430-025-1854-0

Relevance: 0.545 (ignore)

Sedimentary Mn carbonate deposits (SMCDs) constitute the dominant global reservoir of Mn, a critical metal essential for modern technologies, yet their formation timing and links to environmental perturbations remain elusive owing to sparse datable minerals and coarse chronostratigraphic resolution. Here, we report astronomical cycle signals preserved in P (indicative of marine productivity) and (Zr+K)/Al (indicative of detrital flux and relative sea level) from drill core ZK2407 of the Xialei SMCD (∼143 Mt Mn), South China, which is embedded in Late Devonian platform/slope facies strata of the Youjiang Basin. Leveraging the 405 kyr eccentricity cycle and conodont biostratigraphy, the astronomical timescale for the Xialei SMCD was established. The deposition of ore layers I and II+III in the Xialei SMCD took ∼130 and ∼550 kyr, respectively. Ore layer I aligns precisely with the Late Famennian Annulata Event (∼363.4 Ma), while ore layer II+III coincides with the Dasberg Event (∼361.9 Ma). As both mass extinctions were volcanically driven, these temporal couplings suggest large volcanic eruptions as the primary forcing for Mn metallogeny. Massive CO2 emissions from large volcanism induced global warming and sea-level rise, fostering redox-stratified oceanic conditions conducive to SMCD formation. Mn concentrations covary positively in-phase with P and sea-level, indicating transgression-mediated co-transport of anoxic deep-sea hydrothermal Mn(II) and nutrients to the oxic shallow sea, where Mn(III/IV) oxides precipitated. The resulting heightened productivity drove organic matter burial, which subsequently facilitated the reductive transformation of these oxides into Mn carbonates during early diagenesis. Orbital forcing modulated the rhythm of Mn enrichment on astronomical timescales by periodically driving paleoenvironmental variations. This work delineates a temporal framework for the formation of SMCDs and demonstrates that the metallogeny of SMCDs resulted from paleoceanographic changes induced by large volcanism and orbital dynamics.

item.title: The Submarine West Rota Caldera Sulfide Mineral Deposit, Southern Mariana Arc: Exposed roots of a < 1 Ma hydrothermal system
item.dc:creator: Matthew I. Leybourne
James R. Hein
Robert J. Stern
Daniel Layton-Matthews
Yoshihiko Tamura
item.description: We sampled a suite of fresh, altered, and variably mineralized volcanic rocks from West Rota Volcano (WRV) in the Mariana arc. The WRV is an extinct volcano with a 6 × 10 km-wide caldera, the largest in the Mariana arc portion of the Izu-Bonin-Mariana arc system. Samples recovered during dredging and remotely operated vehicle (ROV) dives range from fresh to highly hydrothermally altered rocks (high S and high loss on ignition values) across a compositional range from basalt to dacite. Alteration is characterized by silicification, formation of white mica, and chlorite in some samples, with intense bleaching of rocks. Altered rocks show elevated S (up to 16.9 wt.%), Cu (up to 14.1 wt.%), and various trace metals and metalloids including As, Ag, Bi, Mo, Sb, Se, Te, W, and Pb. We interpret this alteration and enrichment to fluids degassed from an underlying magma chamber interacting in the subsurface with circulating seawater, i.e. magmatic-hydrothermal alteration. Magmatic contributions to alteration are consistent with generally negative δ 34 S VCDT values that range from −15.4 to 2.3 ‰, more negative than is typical for a sediment-free mid-ocean ridge hydrothermal system and consistent with SO 2 disproportionation. We hypothesize that the Cu enrichment in some samples, the intensity and mineralogy of magmatic alteration, and enrichment in magmatic-volatile elements suggests that the samples are from the roots, or stockwork zone, of a seafloor massive sulphide deposit. We suggest that the upper portions of what would have been a massive sulphide deposit (or deposits) were removed by subsequent caldera-forming eruptions and gravity processes. Published: 2026-03-26T15:14:16+00:00 Venue: International Geology Review Score: 0.544 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Matthew I. Leybourne; James R. Hein; Robert J. Stern; Daniel Layton-Matthews; Yoshihiko Tamura

Journal: International Geology Review

DOI: 10.1080/00206814.2026.2644319

Relevance: 0.544 (ignore)

We sampled a suite of fresh, altered, and variably mineralized volcanic rocks from West Rota Volcano (WRV) in the Mariana arc. The WRV is an extinct volcano with a 6 × 10 km-wide caldera, the largest in the Mariana arc portion of the Izu-Bonin-Mariana arc system. Samples recovered during dredging and remotely operated vehicle (ROV) dives range from fresh to highly hydrothermally altered rocks (high S and high loss on ignition values) across a compositional range from basalt to dacite. Alteration is characterized by silicification, formation of white mica, and chlorite in some samples, with intense bleaching of rocks. Altered rocks show elevated S (up to 16.9 wt.%), Cu (up to 14.1 wt.%), and various trace metals and metalloids including As, Ag, Bi, Mo, Sb, Se, Te, W, and Pb. We interpret this alteration and enrichment to fluids degassed from an underlying magma chamber interacting in the subsurface with circulating seawater, i.e. magmatic-hydrothermal alteration. Magmatic contributions to alteration are consistent with generally negative δ 34 S VCDT values that range from −15.4 to 2.3 ‰, more negative than is typical for a sediment-free mid-ocean ridge hydrothermal system and consistent with SO 2 disproportionation. We hypothesize that the Cu enrichment in some samples, the intensity and mineralogy of magmatic alteration, and enrichment in magmatic-volatile elements suggests that the samples are from the roots, or stockwork zone, of a seafloor massive sulphide deposit. We suggest that the upper portions of what would have been a massive sulphide deposit (or deposits) were removed by subsequent caldera-forming eruptions and gravity processes.

item.title: Buoyancy of volatile-rich kimberlite melts, magma ascent, and xenolith transport
item.dc:creator: Anzulović, Ana
Davis. Anne Hope
Gaina, Carmen
Caracas, Razvan
item.description: Kimberlite melts are primary carriers of mantle-derived carbon and hydrogen, playing an important role in Earth’s deep carbon cycle and diamond transport. Their low densities, viscosities, and vapor exsolution enable fast ascent rates. Ascending from the upper mantle, kimberlite melts incorporate xenoliths and xenocrysts and exsolve volatiles. These processes alter their initial composition, increasing the discrepancy between the proto-kimberlite magma and the magma that reaches the surface. To explain kimberlite volcanism, we examine atomic diffusivities and densities of kimberlite melts with varying volatile contents. We show that water makes the melts more diffusive, which should also lower their viscosity. All our kimberlite melts are positively buoyant below the lower continental crust (the MOHO discontinuity). They require ~8.2 wt% CO2 to cross and rise through the MOHO. Above the MOHO, the most volatile-rich kimberlite melts can carry up to ~44% xenolithic fragments of depleted peridotite type. Published: 2026-03-26T14:15:00+00:00 Venue: EarthArXiv Score: 0.533 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Anzulović, Ana; Davis. Anne Hope; Gaina, Carmen; Caracas, Razvan

Journal: EarthArXiv

DOI: 10.31223/X5D19W

Relevance: 0.533 (ignore)

Kimberlite melts are primary carriers of mantle-derived carbon and hydrogen, playing an important role in Earth’s deep carbon cycle and diamond transport. Their low densities, viscosities, and vapor exsolution enable fast ascent rates. Ascending from the upper mantle, kimberlite melts incorporate xenoliths and xenocrysts and exsolve volatiles. These processes alter their initial composition, increasing the discrepancy between the proto-kimberlite magma and the magma that reaches the surface. To explain kimberlite volcanism, we examine atomic diffusivities and densities of kimberlite melts with varying volatile contents. We show that water makes the melts more diffusive, which should also lower their viscosity. All our kimberlite melts are positively buoyant below the lower continental crust (the MOHO discontinuity). They require ~8.2 wt% CO2 to cross and rise through the MOHO. Above the MOHO, the most volatile-rich kimberlite melts can carry up to ~44% xenolithic fragments of depleted peridotite type.
